---
title: "Thread 3 — Optimization, Economics, and Computing as One Phenomenon"
description: "Weatherby's thread, traced through von Neumann / Wald / Savage / Dantzig / RAND / Becker / Friedman / ML / RL / LLMs; Recht's *The Irrational Decision* as the touchstone; Anthropic's Project Vend as a symptom."
order: 3
---

## The animating question

Leif Weatherby's *Automate the C-Suite* opens:

> When Marxists talk about technology, they forget about capitalism. When they talk about capitalism, they forget that technology is embedded in that capitalism.

Weatherby's claim is that both Morozov and Benanav reach for pre-WWII figures (Heidegger, Neurath) and miss what the intervening century actually built: a single linked phenomenon in which optimization mathematics, decision/game theory, computing hardware, neoliberal economic theory, and machine learning are *not separable*. They formed together; they grew together; they are now visible as one apparatus in the form of LLMs and agentic AI.

The genealogy:

- John von Neumann & Oskar Morgenstern, *Theory of Games and Economic Behavior* (1944) — expected utility formalized.
- Abraham Wald — statistical decision functions (1945).
- Jimmy Savage and Milton Friedman — canonical decision theory for economists.
- George Dantzig — Simplex algorithm conceived watching the Berlin Airlift; linear programming; objective functions.
- The CPU itself — von Neumann at Princeton, late 1940s, partly to run Dantzig's optimization problems.
- George Stigler — minimum-acceptable-diet linear program.
- Kenneth Arrow & RAND — social choice; the optimizers' migration through Cold War institutions.
- Gary Becker — *The Economic Approach to Human Behavior* (1976): stable preferences, market equilibrium, *maximizing behavior*. Becker is "deep relativist" about *what* gets maximized (Weatherby's reading). Hence Jameson's "admirably totalizing" line.
- Machine Learning as optimization-over-data: discrimination, classification, generation.
- Reinforcement Learning over chains of reasoning (post-2024) for LLM problem-solving.
- LLMs and agentic AI: optimization plus a hallucinatory cultural surface, returning the apparatus that was once aimed *away from* human culture (Becker excepted) back into it.

The theoretical move that follows from this genealogy, in Weatherby's reading, is the *automation of the C-suite*. The classical Marxist account of automation proletarianizes the worker on the shop floor; the proletarianization of the knowledge worker, however, has nowhere to *split* — the work of the spreadsheet jockey is fundamentally continuous with that of the CEO. Anthropic's Project Vend (the vending-machine-running Claude experiment with the Seymour Cash CEO model) is taken seriously as a *symptom*: the histrionic register of the contemporary wealth class (Ackman tweetstorms, Thiel's gnostic Christianity, AI-doomer fictions) is the affective signature of a class that has been rendered logically vestigial by the very apparatus it presides over.

## What this thread investigates

1. **Reconstruct the genealogy in technical detail.** Not as biography but as a single technical-ideological line. The specific question: *where exactly does the "optimization" framing migrate from one discipline to another, and what does it lose or gain at each crossing?* Wald → Savage is one crossing; Simplex → input-output planning is another; LP → ML risk minimization is another; statistical learning theory → deep learning → LLMs is another. Recht's *The Irrational Decision* is the methodologically careful version of the story this thread wants to write; reading him is the obvious starting point.
2. **Becker as the missing link.** The standard Marxist read of Chicago is "neoclassical price theory plus politics." Weatherby's read is that Becker's relativism about the maximand — let people maximize whatever they want — is what made the apparatus *cultural*, and what enabled the contemporary fusion of Silicon Valley pitch decks with linear programming back-ends. Test this reading by working through Becker (1976) carefully and seeing whether the relativism claim survives.
3. **Project Vend as data, not anecdote.** Anthropic's [Project Vend write-up](https://www.anthropic.com/research/project-vend-1) and the WSJ replication. What did the experiment actually show? Where were the failures, and were they failures of *the model* or of *the C-suite role* the model was placed into? Weatherby's claim that the conclusion ("CEO needs to be well-calibrated") is symptomatic — read it for what it is, not as a model of what should happen but as evidence that the practitioner-class has noticed what their tools imply.
4. **Recht's "when is it safe to optimize?"** Ben Recht's *The Irrational Decision* (forthcoming) argues optimization has been improperly applied to reason itself, to RCTs, and to the digital economy. Trace the specific cases Recht develops and ask which of them touch the planning-LP register from Calculation Course Controversy 2.
5. **The healthcare and university examples.** Weatherby's "doctor as liability-avoiding decision-switch" and the "abolish dean / provost / president" thought experiments. Diagnosis, not policy. But diagnosis of what, exactly? Where do those thought experiments take you if you take them seriously rather than dismissing them?
6. **Where does this leave Cockshott?** Cockshott's planning LP is *also* part of this genealogy. The Marxist who wins the calculation debate by bringing in linear programming has, in Weatherby's frame, also imported the optimization apparatus and its single-criterion structure. Does this thread's reconstruction support Morozov's critique of Benanav, weaken it, or recast it?

## Primary texts

- Weatherby, *Automate the C-Suite* — see [Source texts](../sources.qmd).
- Ben Recht, *The Irrational Decision* (forthcoming, 2026). Recht's blog at <https://www.argmin.net> contains much of the developing argument.
- Paul Erickson, Judy Klein, Lorraine Daston, Rebecca Lemov, Thomas Sturm, Michael Gordin, *How Reason Almost Lost Its Mind: The Strange Career of Cold War Rationality* (Chicago, 2013).
- Anthropic, [Project Vend](https://www.anthropic.com/research/project-vend-1) — the original write-up; supplemented by *Wall Street Journal* coverage of the parallel experiment.

## Adjacent texts

- John von Neumann & Oskar Morgenstern, *Theory of Games and Economic Behavior* (Princeton, 1944).
- Abraham Wald, *Statistical Decision Functions* (Wiley, 1950).
- George Dantzig, *Linear Programming and Extensions* (Princeton, 1963).
- Gary Becker, *The Economic Approach to Human Behavior* (Chicago, 1976).
- Friedrich Hayek, *The Road to Serfdom* (1944) — the contemporaneous reaction to wartime planning that Weatherby flags.
- Wassily Leontief — the input-output framework Dantzig borrowed from for his matrix arithmetic.
- Viktor Mayer-Schönberger & Thomas Ramge, *Reinventing Capitalism in the Age of Big Data* (Basic Books, 2018) — the "data replaces money" hypothesis Weatherby cites.
- Alex Imas's experimental economics work testing whether agent societies endogenously develop money.

## Prospective artifacts

- An annotated genealogy / timeline: a single visual + text artifact that makes the optimization-economics-computing line legible. Possibly a long-form essay; possibly an interactive notebook with a network diagram of which mathematician / department / RAND project / corporation is connected to which.
- A close reading of Project Vend with the source code (if available) and / or a small replication at toy scale, asking the diagnostic question Weatherby asks: not whether agentic AI can run a business, but what the experiment *reveals* about how the capitalist class understands its own role.
- A working paper: *The Single Phenomenon — Optimization, Economics, and Computing in One Frame*. Synthesizes Weatherby + Recht + the genealogy work; probably the longest-running artifact this thread produces.

## Cross-thread connections

- Thread 1 — Buchanan-Vanberg's reframe is what Becker's relativism made *politically* legible; this thread supplies the technical history Thread 1 needs.
- Thread 2 — Benanav's framework, on Weatherby's reading, is genuinely on the "single-criterion" side of capitalism's structure; the multi-criterial form is grafted on top of an optimization core.
- Thread 4 — the labor-market consequences (Mason) are downstream of the technical apparatus this thread reconstructs; Mason's empirical claims about LLM impact map to specific points on this thread's genealogy.
- Thread 6 — blockchain coordination has its own optimization core (consensus mechanisms, MEV optimization, AMM curves); the same genealogy applies, often via Hayekian rhetoric.
